main_summary.summary: Matthew J. Perry Jr., the South Carolina civil rights attorney who later became the state's first African American federal district judge, sits for interviewer Joe Mosnier on 7 June 2011 inside the Matthew J. Perry Jr. US Courthouse in Columbia. Perry traces his trajectory from World War II service in the segregated Quartermaster Corps, including basic training at Camp Van Dorn, Mississippi, through his 1948 entry into the newly created law school at South Carolina State College, opened by South Carolina rather than admit John H. Wrighten III to the all-white University of South Carolina law school. He describes witnessing the spring 1947 federal trials in Columbia of George Elmore (white primaries) and John Wrighten (legal education), both decided by Judge J. Waties Waring, with Thurgood Marshall, Robert L. Carter, and Harold Boulware arguing for the plaintiffs. Perry recounts opening a one-room law office in Spartanburg in 1951, his early municipal-court confrontations with a racist judge, his work on Fleming v. South Carolina Electric and Gas Company (the 1954-1956 bus-segregation case preceding Browder v. Gayle), and his absorption into the NAACP Legal Defense Fund cooperating-attorney network. He attended the Howard moot-court dry run for Brown v. Board of Education.
key_themes: Black legal practice in the segregated South
main_summary.historical_significance:
Perry's first-person account establishes the South Carolina branch of the pre-Brown legal-desegregation effort as a direct eyewitness record. He was physically present in Judge Waring's Columbia courtroom for both Elmore v. Rice (white primaries) and Wrighten v. South Carolina (legal education) in spring 1947, watched Thurgood Marshall and Robert L. Carter try the cases, and traveled to Washington for the Howard University moot-court rehearsal of Brown v. Board. The interview also preserves Perry's testimony that Fleming v. South Carolina Electric and Gas Company predated Browder v. Gayle as a federal-court challenge to intrastate bus segregation, a sequencing point often elided in popular accounts that center Montgomery.

The interview captures an internal architecture of mid-century Southern legal practice that has otherwise gone undocumented: the diploma-privilege provision South Carolina repealed specifically to obstruct graduates of the new SC State law school; the municipal-court practice in which arresting officers served as their own prosecutors; the five-day appeal window Perry used to outflank a hostile judge in Spartanburg; and the division of labor between the NAACP Office of General Counsel and the LDF that was opaque to the lay public but structured cooperating-attorney work.

chapters.title: World War II and Segregated Service
chapters.summary: Perry, born in Columbia in 1921 and a South Carolina State College student when Pearl Harbor was attacked, was drafted in late 1942 and reported in January 1943. He served in the segregated Quartermaster Corps with basic training at Camp Van Dorn, Mississippi, and overseas deployment in England, France, Belgium, and Germany.

chapters.title: Birmingham Terminal Encounter
chapters.summary: On a weekend pass during basic training, Perry traveled by train through Birmingham, Alabama, where he had to order food at a kitchen window outside the terminal restaurant while uniformed Italian prisoners of war were seated and served inside. He frames the encounter as the moment the injustice of segregation became personally legible to him as a uniformed American soldier.
related_events: Italian POW housing in US South

chapters.title: The 1947 Columbia Federal Trials
chapters.summary: Discharged in January 1946, Perry returned to Columbia and as a spectator witnessed two federal trials in spring 1947: George Elmore's challenge to South Carolina's white primary, decided under the Smith v. Allwright precedent, and John Wrighten's suit for admission to the University of South Carolina law school. He identifies the attorneys present: Thurgood Marshall, Robert L. Carter, Harold Boulware, Fred Robinson, and "Squire" Morgan of Orangeburg.

chapters.title: Choosing the Law and Judge Waring's Rulings
chapters.summary: Perry describes his decision to study law, motivated by the Columbia trials and his accumulating recognition of segregation's injustice. He recounts Judge J. Waties Waring's three-fold judgment in Wrighten: South Carolina must either admit Wrighten to USC, provide a separate but substantially equal law school, or cease legal education altogether. South Carolina chose the second option, opening what Thurgood Marshall called a "Jim Crow dump" at South Carolina State College.

chapters.title: Law School and the Diploma Privilege Repeal
chapters.summary: Originally intending to enroll at Howard University, Perry was persuaded to attend the new SC State law school. He describes a small student cohort taught by a Harvard-trained dean and faculty from Columbia, the University of Kansas, and the University of Iowa. South Carolina's legislature, anticipating the new school's graduates, repealed the diploma privilege and required all law graduates to sit for the bar examination beginning with Perry's class of 1951; Perry was the only member of his class to pass.

chapters.title: Briggs v. Elliott and Early Practice
chapters.summary: Within days of graduating, Perry traveled to Charleston in early June 1951 and squeezed into the courtroom to witness the trial of Briggs v. Elliott, the South Carolina case that would be consolidated into Brown v. Board of Education. With no clerkships or government employment available to a Black attorney, he opened a one-room law office in Spartanburg, where Columbia (with three Black attorneys) was already considered "crowded."

chapters.title: Spartanburg Municipal Court Tactics
chapters.summary: Perry recounts a routine pattern in which the Spartanburg municipal judge would impose the maximum sentence (30 days or $100) on his Black clients while letting white-represented defendants off with $10 fines. With no separate prosecutor (the arresting officers prosecuted their own cases), Perry developed and pre-drafted a notice of appeal he could serve immediately upon conviction, eventually using the circuit court appellate process to reverse the judge and shift the local courtroom dynamic.

chapters.title: Union County Habeas Petition
chapters.summary: Called by the Union County Voters League about a young Black man given an excessive sentence by a magistrate judge, Perry received the case on day six, past the five-day appeal window. He filed a petition for a writ of habeas corpus and obtained what he describes as his first victory in the South Carolina Supreme Court.

chapters.title: NAACP Network and the Brown Dry Run
chapters.summary: Perry describes becoming a cooperating attorney with the NAACP Legal Defense Fund while also associating with the NAACP Office of General Counsel, two separate organizations whose distinction was opaque to the public. He traveled to Washington for the Howard University moot-court rehearsal of the Brown v. Board cases, where stand-in justices (Howard faculty and senior attorneys) mimicked the sitting Supreme Court justices' judicial philosophies. He names Thurgood Marshall, Robert L. Carter, Spottswood W. Robinson III, Jack Greenberg, and Constance Baker Motley.
related_events: Brown v. Board Supreme Court arguments

chapters.title: Fleming v. South Carolina Electric and Gas
chapters.summary: Perry recounts Sarah Mae Fleming's 1954 incident on a Columbia city bus operated by the South Carolina Electric and Gas Company, the suit brought by white attorney Phil Wittenberg (whose yard was later burned with a cross), and the case's path through the Fourth Circuit. After Wittenberg withdrew, Perry joined Lincoln Jenkins Jr. at the trial level. He emphasizes that Fleming preceded Browder v. Gayle and the Montgomery bus boycott as a federal challenge to intrastate bus segregation. The interview also references Modjeska Monteith Simkins's role in connecting Fleming with counsel.
related_events: Fleming v. South Carolina Electric and Gas Company (1954-1956)
